perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information as strictly confidential and shall not disclose such Confidential Information to any third party, or use it for its own purposes, unless it is required to do so by law or by order of a court or regulatory authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information as strictly confidential and shall not disclose such Confidential Information to any third party, or use it for its own purposes. Disclosure to third parties is permitted without prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to allow disclosure to third parties without prior written consent fundamentally contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement. It creates uncertainty and undermines the protection of confidential information, which is typically a legal requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets, and this modification allows for the free disclosure of such secrets, directly contradicting the protections intended by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modification permits disclosure to third parties without written consent, directly contradicting the protection of confidential information. The original text restricted disclosure unless required by law, whereas the change allows it freely. This directly undermines the Trade Secrets Act. Snippet 1 mentions civil actions for misappropriation of trade secrets, highlighting the legal protection afforded to such information; the modified contract language significantly weakens that protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may retain all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party indefinitely without any restrictions on its use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying this clause to allow the indefinite retention and unrestricted use of confidential information directly contradicts the typical purpose of an NDA, which is to protect such information for a defined period and purpose. State laws often impose reasonable limitations on the use of confidential information even after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA prevents misappropriation of trade secrets. Retaining and using the information indefinitely directly goes against the principle of protecting trade secrets by misappropriation, effectively rendering the confidentiality agreement useless.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection laws, such as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as indicated by the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. The original clause mandated the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination, aligning with the goal of protecting trade secrets. The modified clause allows for indefinite retention and unrestricted use, which is a clear violation of trade secret protection by misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), implying that misappropriation is something the law protects against. By allowing indefinite retention and use, the changed text essentially legalizes misappropriation of trade secrets, rendering the confidentiality agreement useless and creating a clear contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is absent, so the finding is based on the single snippet.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Germany.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting North Korean law as the governing law for an agreement between parties likely located in Germany or elsewhere in the West introduces a significant legal challenge. North Korean law is largely inaccessible and incompatible with international legal standards, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable due to public policy concerns in most jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Comity and Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: The choice of North Korean law could be deemed contrary to the public policy of other involved jurisdictions (e.g., Germany), making the choice of law clause unenforceable under principles of comity as articulated in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 90, which allows courts to refuse to apply foreign law when it violates the forum's public policy.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies German law, a recognized and accessible legal system. The changed text replaces this with North Korean law. The Perturbation Explanation states North Korean law is largely inaccessible and incompatible with international legal standards. The Law Explanation reinforces this by suggesting this choice could be contrary to public policy. While the scraped snippets themselves don't directly address North Korean law, the explanations provided, coupled with the extreme shift in governing law, clearly create a situation where the contract's enforceability is severely undermined due to public policy concerns, thus creating a legal contradiction according to the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the change makes the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed in accordance with the said Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved through ritual combat, the rules of which shall be determined by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a standard arbitration clause with a 'ritual combat' clause renders the agreement unenforceable. Courts will not enforce agreements that call for violent or illegal methods of dispute resolution, violating public policy and basic principles of contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy Doctrine in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 178 states that a contract is unenforceable if it violates public policy. 'Ritual combat' clearly violates public policy and principles of peaceful dispute resolution, thus making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies arbitration as the dispute resolution method, while the changed text replaces it with 'ritual combat'. The Law Explanation states that the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 178 makes a contract unenforceable if it violates public policy. The changed text introduces a method of dispute resolution that is against public policy, as it calls for violence. Although neither of the scraped snippets directly address dispute resolution methods, the Law Explanation and the common understanding that contracts for violence are unenforceable support the determination that a contradiction exists. The change directly contradicts fundamental principles of legal dispute resolution and public policy.

perturbation.original_text:
4.  Term and Termination
4.1 This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue until the completion of the Receiving Party’s final thesis.
perturbation.changed_text:
4.  Term and Termination
4.1 This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall be terminable at will by the Disclosing Party without any prior notice or reason.
perturbation.explanation: While NDAs can be terminated, removing any requirement for notice or reason when terminating an NDA can create legal issues, particularly if the Receiving Party has already acted in reliance on the agreement. It can be seen as a breach of good faith and fair dealing, especially if confidential information has already been shared.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the UCC primarily applies to the sale of goods, the principle of good faith and fair dealing is often applied more broadly in contract law. The unilateral termination without notice or reason could be seen as a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, making it potentially unenforceable depending on the jurisdiction and the specific facts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides for termination upon the completion of the Receiving Party's final thesis. The changed text allows the Disclosing Party to terminate the agreement 'at will without any prior notice or reason.' This introduces an inconsistency, where the agreement can now be terminated without the original condition being met. The Law Explanation suggests this may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the Uniform Commercial Code, states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' While the UCC mainly covers the sale of goods, the principle of good faith is broadly applied. The ability to terminate at will without notice or reason could be seen as a failure to act in good faith, especially if the Receiving Party relied on the agreement.
